content.text: The Limitation of a Single Head

A single attention head computes one weighted blend of value vectors per position. For a given query, it can only represent one set of attention weights — one "opinion" about which tokens to aggregate. Natural language regularly demands more than one simultaneous perspective: a word may need to track its grammatical subject, its semantic object, and its local phrase context all at once. A single head must spread its weight budget across all these needs, which dilutes every signal.
Subspace Diversity
Each head projects the input into a different
d_k-dimensional subspace

of the original d_model space. The key insight is that heads are not given fixed roles — they discover which subspace is most useful through gradient descent. In practice they converge to different structural roles because it is more efficient to specialise than to generalize.

attrs.alt: Comparison of a single head spreading attention thinly vs three specialised heads each capturing a distinct relationship for the token 'saw'

content.text: The Free Lunch Argument
Using h heads of dimension d_k = d_model/h has the
same total parameter count

as one head of dimension d_model. The split costs nothing in parameters; it only changes how the capacity is allocated. Yet empirically the multi-head version outperforms the single-head equivalent because it can represent multiple independent attention distributions simultaneously.

attrs.latex: \underbrace{h \cdot 3 \cdot d_{\text{model}} \cdot d_k}_{\text{all Q,K,V for }h\text{ heads}} + \underbrace{d_{\text{model}}^2}_{W_O} = 4 d_{\text{model}}^2 \quad \text{(same as 1 head at } d_k = d_{\text{model}}\text{)}

content.text: Empirical Evidence: What Heads Learn
Analysis of trained BERT and GPT models (Clark et al. 2019, Voita et al. 2019) consistently identifies heads that specialise in distinct patterns:
Syntactic heads:
track subject–verb dependencies and noun–modifier relationships. Removing them degrades POS-tagging probes.
Coreference heads:
route pronoun tokens back to their antecedents. Important for tasks like Winograd schema.
Positional heads:
attend to the immediately preceding or following token — functioning like a learned n-gram filter.
Rare-token heads:
strongly attend to rare or unexpected tokens, routing surprising information up the network.
Measuring Head Importance: Ablation
A simple way to measure how much a head contributes is to zero out its output and observe the performance drop:

from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForSequenceClassification
import torch

tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained('bert-base-uncased')
model = AutoModelForSequenceClassification.from_pretrained(
    'bert-base-uncased', num_labels=2)
model.eval()

text = "The food was great but the service was slow."
inputs = tokenizer(text, return_tensors='pt')

# Baseline logits
with torch.no_grad():
    baseline = model(**inputs).logits

# Ablate head 3 in layer 0 by zeroing its output projection rows
def ablate_head(model, layer_idx, head_idx):
    d_model = model.config.hidden_size
    h = model.config.num_attention_heads
    d_k = d_model // h
    # W_O: (d_model, d_model); rows [head_idx*d_k : (head_idx+1)*d_k] correspond to head
    out_proj = model.bert.encoder.layer[layer_idx].attention.self.value
    # Zero the value projection for this head instead (simpler)
    with torch.no_grad():
        start, end = head_idx * d_k, (head_idx + 1) * d_k
        out_proj.weight[start:end, :].zero_()
    return model

ablated_model = ablate_head(model, layer_idx=0, head_idx=3)
with torch.no_grad():
    ablated = ablated_model(**inputs).logits

print("Baseline logits: ", baseline)
print("Ablated logits:  ", ablated)
print("Change:          ", (ablated - baseline).abs().mean().item())
Head Pruning: Many Heads Are Redundant
Voita et al. (2019) showed that in BERT, the majority of heads can be
pruned without measurable accuracy loss

. A small number of heads per layer carry most of the task-relevant signal; the rest provide redundancy or specialise in rare phenomena. This finding motivated structured pruning methods and smaller architectures.

# Conceptual: prune heads with lowest gradient-based importance score
import torch
from transformers import AutoModelForSequenceClassification, AutoTokenizer

model = AutoModelForSequenceClassification.from_pretrained('bert-base-uncased', num_labels=2)
tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained('bert-base-uncased')

# HuggingFace supports head masking natively:
# head_mask: (num_layers, num_heads) tensor of 0/1
num_layers = model.config.num_hidden_layers
num_heads  = model.config.num_attention_heads

# Zero out head 4 in layer 2 and head 7 in layer 5
head_mask = torch.ones(num_layers, num_heads)
head_mask[2, 4] = 0.0
head_mask[5, 7] = 0.0

inputs = tokenizer("Example sentence.", return_tensors='pt')
with torch.no_grad():
    out = model(**inputs, head_mask=head_mask)
print(out.logits)
Optimal Number of Heads

Head count is a hyperparameter. Too few heads under-represents the diversity of linguistic relationships; too many heads each get so few dimensions (d_k = d_model/h) that they lack the capacity to represent complex patterns. Common choices:
Model
d_model
Heads
d_k
BERT-base
BERT-large
GPT-3 175B
LLaMA-3 70B
Across architectures, d_k = 64 or 128 is common — small enough to be fast, large enough for meaningful patterns. The number of heads scales with model size, maintaining the ratio.
Key insight:

Multiple heads are not just an engineering trick — they are why transformers can represent the full combinatorial richness of language. Each head attends to the same sequence but in a different subspace, so the final W_O-combined representation carries information about many relationship types at once.
What Multi-Head Attention Is

Multi-head attention is the core mechanism of the Transformer architecture (Vaswani et al. 2017). Given an input sequence of token representations, each of dimension d_model, multi-head attention computes h independent attention operations in parallel — each in a lower-dimensional subspace of size d_k = d_model / h. The outputs of all heads are concatenated and projected back to d_model via an output matrix W_O. The result is a single vector per position that simultaneously encodes information from multiple different relational perspectives.

Single-head attention computes a weighted sum of value vectors using similarity scores between the query and all key vectors. It can capture one pattern at a time per position. Multi-head attention runs h such operations independently, each with its own Q, K, V projection matrices (W_Q^i, W_K^i, W_V^i). Because these matrices are learned independently, each head converges to a different useful subspace — one might track syntactic dependencies, another semantic similarity, another local phrase context.
Why Multiple Heads Matter

Natural language is relational on multiple dimensions simultaneously. A verb must track its subject (syntactic agreement), its semantic arguments (who does what to whom), temporal markers (tense agreement), and discourse structure (anaphora resolution). Representing all these relationships in a single attention distribution requires the single head to spread its attention budget across all, diluting every signal. Multiple heads each focus on one relationship type, producing much richer per-token representations at no additional parameter cost.

The empirical evidence from probing studies (Clark et al. 2019, Voita et al. 2019, Rogers et al. 2020) confirms this: trained Transformer models develop heads that reliably encode specific linguistic phenomena. Coreference heads route pronouns to their antecedents. Syntactic dependency heads track subject-verb pairs. Positional heads attend to immediately adjacent tokens, functioning as n-gram detectors. This spontaneous specialisation emerges from gradient descent on language modelling objectives alone — the heads are not explicitly assigned roles.
Benefits of Multiple Heads
Parallel relational perspectives.
h heads produce h different attention distributions for the same token, each highlighting a different subset of the sequence. The final W_O projection blends all perspectives into one d_model vector.
Zero parameter overhead.
Using h heads at dimension d_k = d_model/h has the same total parameter count as a single head at dimension d_model. The 4d_model² parameter budget is constant regardless of head count.
Implicit redundancy and robustness.

Pruning experiments show that individual heads can be removed without significant accuracy loss — the remaining heads compensate. This built-in redundancy makes Transformers robust to head-level failures.
Interpretability.

Individual heads have been shown to encode interpretable linguistic patterns, making multi-head attention partially amenable to mechanistic interpretability analysis — probing heads for specific functions.
Trade-offs and Limitations

More heads do not unconditionally improve performance. If h is too large, each head's subspace dimensionality d_k = d_model/h becomes too small to represent complex patterns. A head with d_k = 8 dimensions cannot encode rich semantic relationships that require 64+ dimensions. BERT-base uses h = 12 with d_k = 64; models with very high head counts at fixed d_model produce diminishing returns.
Attention is O(n²) in sequence length.

Each head computes full pairwise attention over all positions. With h heads, the attention cost is h × O(n²), but since h is constant and small relative to n, the bottleneck is still sequence length squared. Long-context models (>32K tokens) require efficient attention variants (FlashAttention, sparse attention).
Most heads are redundant post-training.

Structured pruning consistently shows that >50% of heads can be removed without significant performance loss on downstream tasks. This implies the training process does not efficiently utilise all heads — capacity is wasted.
No explicit control over head specialisation.

Heads learn their roles through gradient descent with no explicit supervision. The patterns that emerge depend on architecture and training data, making it difficult to guarantee specific heads learn desired relationship types.
When Head Count Matters in Practice

For most practitioners using off-the-shelf Transformer architectures, the head count is fixed by the model choice. When designing custom Transformer architectures, the d_k = 64 convention (used by BERT-base and most standard models) is a well-validated starting point. Below d_k = 32, heads struggle to represent complex patterns; above d_k = 128, marginal benefit per head decreases while memory per head increases.

Grouped-query attention (GQA) and multi-query attention (MQA) are variants used in inference-optimised models (LLaMA 2, Mistral). In GQA, multiple query heads share K and V projections, reducing KV cache memory without significantly reducing model quality. This is not about reducing the number of attention perspectives but about reducing the memory bandwidth required to store cached keys and values during autoregressive decoding.
Implementing Multi-Head Attention from Scratch

import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.nn.functional as F
import math

class MultiHeadAttention(nn.Module):
    """Vanilla multi-head attention (Vaswani et al. 2017)."""

    def __init__(self, d_model: int, n_heads: int, dropout: float = 0.1):
        super().__init__()
        assert d_model % n_heads == 0, "d_model must be divisible by n_heads"
        self.d_model = d_model
        self.n_heads = n_heads
        self.d_k = d_model // n_heads

        # Single weight matrix for all heads (concatenated): shape (d_model, d_model)
        self.W_Q = nn.Linear(d_model, d_model, bias=False)
        self.W_K = nn.Linear(d_model, d_model, bias=False)
        self.W_V = nn.Linear(d_model, d_model, bias=False)
        self.W_O = nn.Linear(d_model, d_model, bias=False)
        self.dropout = nn.Dropout(dropout)

    def split_heads(self, x: torch.Tensor) -> torch.Tensor:
        """(B, T, d_model) → (B, h, T, d_k)."""
        B, T, _ = x.shape
        return x.view(B, T, self.n_heads, self.d_k).transpose(1, 2)

    def forward(
        self,
        query: torch.Tensor,   # (B, T_q, d_model)
        key:   torch.Tensor,   # (B, T_k, d_model)
        value: torch.Tensor,   # (B, T_k, d_model)
        mask:  torch.Tensor | None = None,  # (B, 1, T_q, T_k) or broadcastable
    ) -> tuple[torch.Tensor, torch.Tensor]:
        B = query.size(0)

        # Project and split into h heads
        Q = self.split_heads(self.W_Q(query))  # (B, h, T_q, d_k)
        K = self.split_heads(self.W_K(key))    # (B, h, T_k, d_k)
        V = self.split_heads(self.W_V(value))  # (B, h, T_k, d_k)

        # Scaled dot-product attention: (B, h, T_q, T_k)
        scale = math.sqrt(self.d_k)
        scores = torch.matmul(Q, K.transpose(-2, -1)) / scale

        if mask is not None:
            scores = scores.masked_fill(mask == 0, float('-inf'))

        attn_weights = self.dropout(F.softmax(scores, dim=-1))  # (B, h, T_q, T_k)

        # Weighted sum of values per head: (B, h, T_q, d_k)
        context = torch.matmul(attn_weights, V)

        # Concatenate heads: (B, T_q, d_model)
        context = context.transpose(1, 2).contiguous().view(B, -1, self.d_model)

        # Final projection
        out = self.W_O(context)   # (B, T_q, d_model)
        return out, attn_weights  # return weights for visualisation


# Usage
d_model, n_heads, seq_len, batch = 512, 8, 32, 4
mha = MultiHeadAttention(d_model, n_heads)
x = torch.randn(batch, seq_len, d_model)
out, weights = mha(x, x, x)  # self-attention
print("Output shape:", out.shape)        # (4, 32, 512)
print("Weights shape:", weights.shape)   # (4, 8, 32, 32) — h attention maps

# Inspect what each head attends to most strongly
head_0_mean_attention = weights[0, 0].mean(dim=0)   # (32,) — avg attention received
print("Head 0 top-attended positions:", head_0_mean_attention.topk(3).indices.tolist())
Real-world Example: Head Specialisation in BERT

Research on BERT (Clark et al. 2019) used gradient-based attribution to identify heads responsible for specific linguistic phenomena. Head 8-10 was found to strongly attend to direct object relationships; heads in early layers attended predominantly to adjacent tokens (positional heads); heads in middle layers tracked syntactic dependencies. The following code reproduces a simple version of this analysis using the HuggingFace Transformers library:

from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModel
import torch

tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained("bert-base-uncased")
model = AutoModel.from_pretrained("bert-base-uncased", output_attentions=True)
model.eval()

sentence = "The cat that the dog chased was very fast."
inputs = tokenizer(sentence, return_tensors="pt")
tokens = tokenizer.convert_ids_to_tokens(inputs["input_ids"][0])

with torch.no_grad():
    outputs = model(**inputs)

# outputs.attentions: tuple of (1, n_heads, seq_len, seq_len) per layer
# BERT-base: 12 layers, 12 heads each
all_attentions = torch.stack(outputs.attentions)  # (12, 1, 12, T, T)

def analyse_head(layer: int, head: int):
    """Show the top-3 attended tokens for each query position."""
    attn = all_attentions[layer, 0, head]  # (T, T)
    print(f"\nLayer {layer}, Head {head}:")
    for i, token in enumerate(tokens):
        top = attn[i].topk(3)
        attended = [(tokens[j], f"{w:.2f}") for j, w in zip(top.indices.tolist(), top.values.tolist())]
        print(f"  {token:15} → {attended}")

# Layer 0 positional heads (attend to adjacent tokens)
analyse_head(0, 0)

# Layer 6 — tends to capture longer-range syntactic patterns
analyse_head(6, 3)

# Layer 11 — deep semantic / coreference patterns
analyse_head(11, 7)
Common Mistakes
Not scaling attention scores by √d_k.

Without the √d_k scaling factor, dot products grow in magnitude proportional to d_k as dimensions increase, pushing softmax into saturated regions where gradients vanish. The scaling keeps dot products in a moderate range regardless of d_k.
Using d_k > d_model / n_heads.

Some implementations incorrectly set d_k independently of n_heads. The standard convention is d_k = d_model / n_heads to preserve the parameter budget equivalence between single-head and multi-head. Using larger d_k multiplies parameter count.
Forgetting to reshape attention weights before inspection.

Attention weights from HuggingFace models are returned as (batch, n_heads, T, T). Indexing [0, head_idx] gives the attention matrix for one head in the first batch item. Averaging over the head dimension gives average behaviour but loses per-head specialisation.
Over-interpreting attention as explanation.

High attention weight does not necessarily mean causal importance. Gradient-based attribution (integrated gradients, attention × gradient) provides stronger evidence of which attention connections are functionally important.
Best Practices
Keep d_k = 64 as a starting point.
This is the value used in BERT-base (768/12) and most successful Transformers. It provides sufficient representational capacity while keeping memory per head manageable.
Use FlashAttention for long sequences.

Standard attention materialises the full (T × T) attention matrix in GPU memory, which is O(T²) in space. FlashAttention computes the same result in O(T) space by tiling and fusing the softmax computation, enabling context lengths of 32K–128K tokens.
Investigate GQA for inference-heavy workloads.

Grouped-query attention (GQA) reduces KV cache memory during autoregressive decoding by sharing key/value projections across groups of query heads. LLaMA 2 and Mistral use GQA to achieve better inference throughput without significant quality loss.
Performance and Scalability

The computational cost of multi-head attention is dominated by two terms: the attention weight matrix computation (QKᵀ / √d_k), which is O(n² · d_k · h) = O(n² · d_model) per layer; and the value aggregation (attn · V), also O(n² · d_model). The projection matrices W_Q, W_K, W_V, W_O are O(n · d_model²) operations. For short sequences (n ≪ d_model), the projection matrices dominate; for long sequences (n ≫ d_model), the attention matrix dominates.

attrs.latex: \text{FLOPs}_{\text{attention}} \approx 4 n^2 d_{\text{model}} + 8 n d_{\text{model}}^2 \quad (\text{per Transformer layer})

content.text: For BERT-base (d_model=768, h=12, n=512), the attention FLOPs per layer are approximately 1.2B for the sequence term and 3.0B for the projection term, confirming that projections dominate at typical sequence lengths. At n=4096, the quadratic term surpasses projections and becomes the bottleneck — motivating efficient attention variants.
Tooling and Ecosystem
torch.nn.MultiheadAttention.
PyTorch's built-in implementation, supporting batch-first format, causal masks, and key-padding masks. Uses fused CUDA kernels for efficiency.
FlashAttention-2 (Dao et al. 2023).
A drop-in replacement for standard attention with O(n) GPU memory usage and 2-4x speedup. Available via flash-attn package and integrated in HuggingFace Transformers with use_flash_attention_2=True.
BertViz.
Interactive attention visualisation for BERT and other HuggingFace models. Renders per-head and aggregated attention matrices, useful for understanding head specialisation in a trained model.
Transformer Lens (EleutherAI).
A mechanistic interpretability library for decomposing Transformer computations into individual heads, MLP layers, and residual stream contributions. Enables systematic head-level analysis.
Security and Reliability

Attention patterns are exploitable in adversarial settings. Adversarial suffix attacks (Zou et al. 2023) work partly by manipulating attention distributions — injected tokens receive high attention weight from the model's query heads, redirecting information flow and overriding the original instruction. Multi-head attention's global receptive field means that adversarial tokens anywhere in the context can influence all subsequent tokens' representations.

From a reliability perspective, numerical stability in attention computation requires the softmax temperature to remain finite. When attention logits are very large (due to poor initialisation or training without scaling), softmax outputs become one-hot, and gradients vanish. Pre-LayerNorm architectures (placing LayerNorm before attention) are more numerically stable than Post-LayerNorm during early training.
Key Takeaways
Multi-head attention runs h independent attention operations in parallel, each in a d_k = d_model/h dimensional subspace. The outputs are concatenated and projected via W_O.
Using h heads at d_k = d_model/h costs the same number of parameters (4d_model²) as one head at d_k = d_model — the representation gain is free in terms of capacity.

Empirically, different heads spontaneously specialise: syntactic dependency heads, coreference heads, positional heads, and rare-token heads emerge through gradient descent on language modelling objectives alone.
Most heads in trained models are redundant — over 50% can be pruned without significant accuracy loss. The multi-head design provides built-in robustness and redundancy.
Always scale attention scores by 1/√d_k to prevent softmax saturation and gradient vanishing as d_k grows.
For long sequences (>4K tokens), use FlashAttention to reduce the quadratic memory cost of materialising the full attention matrix.
For inference-heavy deployments, Grouped-Query Attention (GQA) reduces KV-cache memory by sharing key/value projections across groups of heads, improving throughput without significant quality loss.
